phrases.meaning: I don't care. / It's all the same to me.
phrases.situation: A: Sollen wir ins Kino oder ins Restaurant gehen? B: Das ist mir Wurst, ich bin mit beidem zufrieden. (A: Should we go to the movies or a restaurant? B: I don't care, I'm happy with either.)

phrases.meaning: This is the decisive moment; everything is at stake.
phrases.situation: Said before the final match of a tournament: Jetzt geht es um die Wurst — wer heute gewinnt, kommt ins Finale. (Now it's crunch time — whoever wins today goes to the final.)

phrases.meaning: To give one's (often unwanted) opinion.
phrases.situation: In a meeting where someone keeps interrupting: Er muss immer seinen Senf dazugeben, auch wenn ihn niemand gefragt hat. (He always has to put his two cents in, even when nobody asked.)

phrases.meaning: Now we're in a mess; now look what's happened.
phrases.situation: After forgetting the tickets at home: Jetzt haben wir den Salat — ohne Tickets kommen wir nicht rein! (Now we're in trouble — without tickets we can't get in!)

phrases.meaning: That's not my problem / none of my business.
phrases.situation: A: Die Nachbarn streiten schon wieder. B: Na ja, das ist nicht mein Bier. (A: The neighbors are arguing again. B: Well, that's not my business.)

phrases.meaning: I don't understand a thing; it's all Greek to me.
phrases.situation: Listening to a tax form explanation: Bei diesem Steuerformular verstehe ich nur Bahnhof. (With this tax form I don't understand a single thing.)

phrases.literal: That's where the dog is buried.
phrases.meaning: That's the real problem / the crux of the matter.
phrases.situation: After finally finding the bug in the code: Ah, da liegt der Hund begraben — die Datei wurde nie gespeichert! (Ah, that's the real problem — the file was never saved!)

phrases.literal: To have tomatoes on one's eyes
phrases.meaning: To be oblivious to something obvious right in front of you.
phrases.situation: A: Wo ist denn der Schlüssel? Er liegt doch direkt vor dir! B: Oh, ich hatte wohl Tomaten auf den Augen. (A: Where's the key? It's right in front of you! B: Oh, I must have been blind to it.)

phrases.literal: To step into the little pot of fat
phrases.meaning: To make an embarrassing social blunder.
phrases.situation: After asking a colleague about her age at a party: Er ist mal wieder ins Fettnäpfchen getreten und hat sie nach ihrem Alter gefragt. (He put his foot in it again and asked her how old she was.)

phrases.literal: To buy the cat in the sack
phrases.meaning: To buy something without inspecting it first; a pig in a poke.
phrases.situation: Buying a used car online without seeing it: Kauf das Auto nicht ungesehen — sonst kaufst du die Katze im Sack! (Don't buy the car sight unseen — otherwise you're buying a pig in a poke!)

phrases.literal: To talk around the hot porridge
phrases.meaning: To beat around the bush instead of getting to the point.
phrases.situation: A: Sag mir einfach, was los ist! Red nicht um den heißen Brei herum. (A: Just tell me what's going on! Stop beating around the bush.)

phrases.meaning: That's where the party's really happening.
phrases.situation: Talking about a friend's birthday party: Geh unbedingt zu Toms Party, da steppt der Bär! (You have to go to Tom's party, that's where it's really happening!)

phrases.meaning: To be lucky, to get away with something.
phrases.situation: Just missing a fine: Der Kontrolleur kam eine Minute nach mir — da habe ich echt Schwein gehabt! (The inspector came a minute after me — I really got lucky there!)

phrases.meaning: To be fed up with something.
phrases.situation: After a third meeting cancellation: Ich habe echt die Nase voll von diesen ständigen Terminänderungen! (I'm really fed up with these constant schedule changes!)

phrases.meaning: To get on someone's nerves.
phrases.situation: About a coworker who keeps interrupting: Ehrlich, seine ständigen Kommentare gehen mir echt auf den Keks. (Honestly, his constant comments really get on my nerves.)

phrases.meaning: That's very easy, a piece of cake.
phrases.situation: A: Ist die neue Software schwer zu bedienen? B: Nein, das ist ein Kinderspiel. (A: Is the new software hard to use? B: No, it's a piece of cake.)

phrases.meaning: Good luck! / Break a leg!
phrases.situation: Before a friend's job interview: Viel Erfolg heute — Hals- und Beinbruch! (Good luck today — break a leg!)

phrases.literal: To be able to steal horses with someone
phrases.meaning: To trust someone completely; to be up for any adventure together.
phrases.situation: Describing an old, reliable friendship: Mit ihr kann man Pferde stehlen — sie hilft immer, egal was passiert. (She's someone you can count on for anything — she always helps, no matter what.)

phrases.meaning: Everything's fine, all good.
phrases.situation: A: Wie läuft's mit dem Umzug? B: Alles in Butter, wir sind schon fast fertig! (A: How's the move going? B: All good, we're almost done!)

phrases.literal: To tie a bear onto someone
phrases.meaning: To trick someone / tell them a tall tale.
phrases.situation: A: Er hat erzählt, er sei mal Astronaut gewesen. B: Der hat dir einen Bären aufgebunden! (A: He said he used to be an astronaut. B: He was pulling your leg!)

phrases.meaning: To keep one's fingers crossed for someone.
phrases.situation: Before a friend's exam: Ich drücke dir die Daumen für die Prüfung morgen! (I'll keep my fingers crossed for your exam tomorrow!)

phrases.phrase: Nicht das Gelbe vom Ei sein
phrases.literal: To not be the yellow of the egg
phrases.meaning: To not be the best, not ideal.
phrases.situation: Reviewing a mediocre restaurant: Das Essen war okay, aber wirklich nicht das Gelbe vom Ei. (The food was okay, but really nothing special.)

phrases.phrase: Aus einer Mücke einen Elefanten machen
phrases.literal: To make an elephant out of a mosquito
phrases.meaning: To make a mountain out of a molehill; to exaggerate a small problem.
phrases.situation: A: Er ist total wütend wegen der kleinen Verspätung. B: Der macht wieder aus einer Mücke einen Elefanten. (A: He's furious about the small delay. B: He's making a mountain out of a molehill again.)

phrases.phrase: Sich etwas hinter die Ohren schreiben
phrases.literal: To write something behind one's ears
phrases.meaning: To take good note of something and remember it well (often a warning).
phrases.situation: After being warned about a rule at work: Das solltest du dir wirklich hinter die Ohren schreiben, sonst passiert es dir nochmal. (You should really take note of that, or it'll happen to you again.)

phrases.phrase: Alles hat ein Ende, nur die Wurst hat zwei.
phrases.literal: Everything has one end, only the sausage has two.
phrases.meaning: A playful way of saying that everything eventually comes to an end.
phrases.situation: Wrapping up a long project: Na, alles hat ein Ende, nur die Wurst hat zwei — morgen ist das Projekt endlich fertig! (Well, all good things come to an end — tomorrow the project is finally finished!)
